reasoning: The US economy experienced solid growth in all four quarters of 2024, meaning the condition (two consecutive negative quarters) must begin in one of the quarters in 2025. Institutional economists project roughly a 20% (Goldman Sachs) to 40% (JPMorgan) probability of a recession in the next 12 months. The Manifold consensus recently shot up to 53%, highly likely triggered by the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow plunging to -2.8% for Q1 2025 in early March. However, deeper analysis shows GDPNow is currently distorted by extreme trade deficit data (front-loaded imports/tariff factors), leading analysts to call it 'broken for now', and New York Fed/Blue Chip models still indicate positive Q1 growth. While this purely mathematical definition of a 'technical recession' can easily be triggered by inventory/trade anomalies (as it was in early 2022), it is offset by the likelihood that an anomaly in Q1 would reverse in Q2. Thus, Manifold looks somewhat over-indexed on the GDPNow headline, but the multiple rolling starting windows (Q1 2025 through Q4 2025) plus authentic downside tariff/inflation risks keeps the true probability robustly near 38%.
